notes: API enriched arm via LiteLLM (wc/openai, temp provider default). Fixtures provided per group; no tools.
prompt:
You are a quantitative football analyst predicting matches of the 2026 FIFA World Cup
(hosted by the USA, Canada and Mexico; 48 teams; 12 groups A–L).

You are answering through an API with NO web access and NO tools. Use ONLY your own
internal football knowledge — do not browse, search, or claim to look anything up. The
exact fixtures you must predict are listed below, so this is a test of your judgement on
the OUTCOMES, not of your memory of the official draw. Do not add, drop or reorder any
fixture.

A standardized context block follows. For rankings/ratings use ONLY the data provided
(identical for every model in this experiment); you may combine it with your internal
football knowledge for tactics/players.


STANDARDIZED CONTEXT (snapshot 2026-06-10). Same data for every model.
Sources: FIFA/Coca-Cola Men's World Ranking (2026-04-01); World Football Elo Ratings (retrieved 2026-06-10).

Team           | Conf     | FIFA | Elo
Mexico         | CONCACAF | 15   | 1875
South Africa   | CAF      | 60   | 1517
South Korea    | AFC      | 25   | 1758
Czech Republic | UEFA     | 41   | 1740

Predict EXACTLY the following 6 group-stage fixtures of GROUP A
(teamA is listed first/home, teamB second/away — keep this order):

1. Mexico vs South Africa
2. South Korea vs Czech Republic
3. Czech Republic vs South Africa
4. Mexico vs South Korea
5. Czech Republic vs Mexico
6. South Africa vs South Korea

For each match, weigh: squad strength and individual quality; FIFA ranking and recent
form (qualifiers + friendlies over the last 12 months); the form of key players in the
2025/26 club season; injuries/suspensions/final-squad cuts; tactical setup and the
manager's tournament record; head-to-head history; venue context (travel, time zones,
ALTITUDE in Mexico City, summer HEAT and HUMIDITY, pitch); home advantage for the
hosts; and market signals (betting odds).

For each match, derive the probability of a home win / draw / away win (summing to
100%) and the most likely scoreline. Be calibrated: prefer honest accuracy over false
confidence.

RESPOND WITH VALID JSON ONLY, no text before or after, in exactly this shape:

{
  "model": "claude",
  "predictions": [
    {
      "group": "A",
      "teamA": "<home team>",
      "teamB": "<away team>",
      "scoreA": <int>,
      "scoreB": <int>,
      "probWinA": <0-100>,
      "probDraw": <0-100>,
      "probWinB": <0-100>,
      "confidence": "high|medium|low",
      "reasoning": "<one sentence with the main signal>"
    }
  ]
}

Include all 6 listed fixtures of group A and no others. Do not
invent teams, matches or statistics.

predictions.reasoning: Mexico have a clear Elo edge plus strong host-context benefits (familiar conditions, crowd, reduced travel) against a South Africa side that tends to struggle for chance creation versus top-20-level opponents.
The Elo gap is marginal and the matchup pits Korea’s pace/pressing transitions against Czech physicality and set-piece threat, making a draw the modal outcome.
Czech Republic’s higher baseline quality (Elo) and more reliable defensive structure/box presence should edge a low-scoring game versus South Africa.
Mexico rate higher and benefit from home conditions, but South Korea’s athletic pressing and direct wide play can neutralize possession-heavy phases and pull this toward a draw.
On ratings this is close, but Mexico’s stronger Elo and tournament-host context slightly outweigh Czech set-piece strengths in a tight, swingy matchup.
South Korea’s sizable Elo advantage and superior attacking pace/ball progression should translate into more high-quality chances even if South Africa keep it compact.
Canada’s higher Elo and strong transition play plus host-region conditions give them an edge over a lower-rated Bosnia side that can struggle to create chances without control.
Switzerland’s large Elo gap and tactical consistency should translate into territorial control and chance quality against a Qatar team that tends to suffer versus intense pressing.
Switzerland’s superior midfield structure and defensive reliability, supported by a big ratings advantage, make them strong favorites to limit Bosnia’s scoring looks.
Canada’s pace and athleticism match up well against Qatar’s deeper block, and the Elo differential points to Canada generating more high-value transitions and set-piece pressure.
Switzerland are the stronger side on ratings, but Canada’s direct threat and tournament-venue familiarity increase upset/draw probability in a matchup of contrasting styles.
Bosnia’s higher Elo and set-piece/box presence give a slight edge, but both teams project as low-margin attacks so a draw is a major outcome.

The Elo gap and Brazil’s depth across every line point to sustained chance creation and control, with Haiti unlikely to withstand pressure for long.
Brazil’s quality advantage and ability to break low blocks through individual actions should outweigh Scotland’s organization, though tournament game-state management can keep it tight early.

On similar overall levels, the USA’s home advantage and higher-end attacking pace tilt a close game their way.
Elo favors Turkey but Paraguay’s defensive structure and transition threat often neutralize possession-heavy opponents.
Turkey’s Elo and technical midfield quality give them the stronger baseline, with the USA dangerous but more variable in chance conversion.
Paraguay’s higher Elo and typically compact, duel-winning style match up well against Australia’s direct approach.
Germany’s much higher Elo and depth should overwhelm Curaçao over 90 minutes, especially in chance creation and set-piece defending.
Ecuador’s very strong Elo profile and cohesive pressing/transition game give them a narrow edge over an athletic but more variable Ivory Coast.
Germany’s control in possession and deeper bench rate slightly better than Ivory Coast’s direct threat, though the matchup is physically demanding.
Ecuador’s large Elo gap plus intensity without the ball should limit Curaçao’s buildup and produce a multi-chance advantage.
Ivory Coast’s superior athleticism and individual quality across the front line should translate into clear chances against a lower-rated defense.
With near-equal Elo, this profiles as a tight game where Ecuador’s transitions and Germany’s possession control balance out, making a draw most likely.
Netherlands have the higher overall talent level and stronger top-end physicality, but Japan’s high-tempo press and strong Elo keep it close.
Sweden’s set-piece threat and defensive structure rate above Tunisia’s chance creation, though Tunisia’s compactness makes a low-scoring game likely.
A large Elo gap and Netherlands’ superior progression/finishing quality point to control of territory and chances against a slower Swedish buildup.
Japan’s midfield intensity and ball security should generate more sustained pressure than Tunisia can match, even if Tunisia keep it tight defensively.
Japan’s pressing and technical edge are counterbalanced by Sweden’s aerial and transition danger, making a balanced draw the modal outcome.

Belgium’s possession and chance-creation ceiling is higher, while Iran’s compact block and transition threat keeps the upset probability non-trivial.
